item.title: You sideways assholes
item.content:encoded: An easy +1! Not so much for the SCP as for the excellent way it was written. And the sideways assholes epithet. By the way… when I saw the full name of Peter Panepi I thought I was in for the weirdest reboot of a Disney character ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm actually a big fan of Scranton Reality Anchors, because some skips make no sense without them. If you've got something that can, say, teleport unlimited distances or rewind time, there's no variant of "put it in a box with thick walls" that seems at all believable. And as fun as they are in moderation, I would really prefer we don't require ancient Kabbalistic rituals or friendly Class 9999 reality warpers restraining all skips of this caliber.

Also, "Reality Anchor" gives a pretty decent idea of what the device does in general.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article is set up in such a way that that link would normally be a collapsible with more stuff in it. It's a trick to get you to click the link and go to the next iteration of the document and continue the narrative, but the footnotes are part of what would've been in the collapsible if it was openable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty good article overall. Question about the footnotes though: The initial page and the first iteration pages both have 5 footnotes listed at the bottom, but only 3 used in the article. Is there a reason for this, or is it some sort of error? Still figuring out the wiki formatting, so I'm not sure if it's part of the article that it's hidden or if something was missed in the coding.

item.title: STAFF POST - OPEN Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Show Reason
Hide Reason

The following crosslinks are being suggested based on their relation to A) Another article featuring the same GOI mentioned in this one, and B) SCP-2280 has a pending proposal for crosslinking another article due to speaking coptic, since 2343 would speak this language as well, a crosslink along the same lines from 2343 to 2280 would be appropriate.
Editing this:

This is believed to be a reference to GOI-13, The Journeymen.

and

Due to the object's voluntary containment, the continued cooperation of SCP-2343 is a top priority.

To this:

This is believed to be a reference to GOI-13, The Journeymen.

and

Due to the object's voluntary containment, the continued cooperation of SCP-2343 is a top priority.1

Footnotes
1. Use of SCP-2343 as a possible translator for SCP-2280 is currently pending review.

item.title: What do you know, a good 343.
item.content:encoded:
This is how you write a reality-bender, folks. Very satisfying read.

(Accidentally replied instead of making a new post… That's Wikidot for you. Oops!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Im not allowed to downvote this purely because it did not tell me how to get to Memphis, am I?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm aware this is extremely old, and this is just for future folks who look through the old discussions -

But the Foundation caught the edits, and that edits were made is itself useful information to retain - so that it's known the skip can pull it off. Slap on a warning, make sure the real document is available, and it's about as good as you can hope.

A sufficiently determined attacker can, with sufficient time and access, defeat any security, but good security makes sure to alert somebody when it happens.

item.title: American Gods Pt. 1
item.content:encoded:
I feel that if the SCP Foundation took place in Gaiam's world of Anicent Gods in America, this would be part of the formula. I don't know how it would go, probably lead to some kind of epic war between Old and New Gods with the Foundation in the middle.

Keep up the good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: By the time the police got there he was in the road.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
police were called to a domestic disturbance

domestic

officers eventually took the subject into custody due to public intoxication

public

So…which was it? Is it not one or the other, a private residence or a public place?

item.title: This is the story all about how
item.content:encoded:
Pretty good scip. In terms of originality, I'd say reality bending has been done before. (At least I think, not a veteran around here so I can't say.)
But this one has a nice tone and narrative to it. Reminds me of a Christopher Nolan movie for some reason. And the picture of SCP 2343 actually kind of looks like someone I knew. But I like this SCP.

Gold star! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I didn't notice that.

Cimmerian, you sly dog you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you look closely he also edited his height and weight a bit. The details!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 to this one. Only criticisms are the use of SRAs and the fact that the twist was too subtle. I had to go to the discussion page to understand what was going on. That said, the idea of a super-powerful but friendly being messing with the SCP article for the sake of…well, ego (the picture) and to notify the Foundation of his psycho wife? I love it.

Just wish it'd been Odin and Frigg instead. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
2343 is facist killer confirmed.

Guess that's a good thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: nice

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The format screw works and I upvote hesitantly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've never said this here for some reason, but this is one of my favorite scips. I've shown this to a lot of my non-scp friends. It tells an interesting story and is also just enough of a format screw to get people interested. +1 forever, bb.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this skip. All three iterations of it! Also, with or without the spoiler provided by the author. Subtle, original, fun, creepy. Off to upvote all three!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had him show up as a white guy because he showed up in the south in the 1960's originally. When he first manifested he was ancient Egyptian. I like to think he saw a trucker on some lonely abandoned road and took on his appearance because he wanted to fit in better.

As for "that bitch" I've edited it out. I liked it originally but I sorta took the third page in a different direction that makes it out of place now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Having read the spoilers, I don't like that he's randomly changed to a white guy (even tho I like that pic, and if I were writing this, I'd keep the cowboy part even if not the ethnicity), nor do I like the pointless and cliche'd "that bitch" bits in the second document —

— but aside from that, I love all of this, especially the final entry. Nice. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

…and that kids is how I got To Memphis

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone needs to write a tale about 2343 and 208 meeting before being contained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure about this one either. I personally didnt like SCP-343 either, writing God into an SCP just seems extremely lazy to me. I appreciate the personality you've given the character, but it all seems a little disjointed. I'm not an Egypt nerd and the only understanding of God's I have is in relation to the humans that created them, maybe I just dont find this kind of fiction interesting because I've seen it done before sooooo many times. Its a God, not an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… I had no clue.

Even then, how many non-military or non-gun enthusiasts would know it and not assume it was a Foundation thing like I had?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: close-in weapons systems are a real thing, you know

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The overall problem here is that Reality Anchors do not adhere to this tip.

What the hell is a HMCL? What's a CIW System? What's a class 3 buckle harness? What is Procedure 110-Montauk? What is a Mobile Task Force? What is the O5 Council? What is the Ethics Committee? Where does one find all the documents referred to in an article?

Point is, the Reality Anchor is an extension of the worldbuilding process that has existed in SCP articles from the very start. To say that this article fails the stand-alone clause because of Reality Anchors means that a large majority of articles would also fail that very same clause. Many of those articles hold the appearance that specific knowledge of things not expanded on can be found elsewhere on the site, or refer to things that a first-time reader will not know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, they have the in universe science behind it. Type in "humes" to the search bar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, there's an "explained" designation specifically for that reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of things I look for in SCPs is a tip found in "How to Write An SCP" Guide. It states the following:

"Act as if every SCP will be the first that someone will read. That means do not put too much in there that requires knowledge of anything else on the site. While many people enjoy linking things together into a larger story, it really improves the quality of the work when each SCP can be enjoyed in full as a stand-alone work."

The overall problem here is that Reality Anchors do not adhere to this tip. It makes it so others have to go around and try to find the articles (or may not even know there were other articles explaining them; like my case) and it just kind of clutters the readers understanding if they have to jump around figuring stuff out that is not in the article itself.

But perhaps my downvote was misguided and my problem lies with something more encompassing. Ill switch to neutral for now.

@djkaktus It is not just a piece of equipment, otherwise you could say that about half the equipment/machine/technological based SCPs. You could call SCP-914 just a piece a equipment that does something. The Foundation could probably spend the time to figure out exactly the fictional science/technology behind it, and maybe reproduce it. Would it then not be an SCP anymore?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I, for one, love made up devices. Like, the P.K.E. Meter from Ghostbusters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At this point, it seems that anamolies have just as much control over Foundation records as the Foundation does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Certainly your own opinion and you're entitled to it, but as Decibelle explained below, Kant Counters and Scranton Reality Anchors are a real thing and have been a pretty major part of site lore for a while now.

Additionally, SCPs that contain SCPs are also a real thing, and they're called Thaumiel-class objects. What you're describing, however, is just a piece of equipment, and not a skip.

To put it in the words of somebody blah blah who said something once, "sufficiently advanced technology will always look like magic to the uninformed."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The reality anchors were first used in SCP-1451, and have been used in multiple pieces since, most notably SCP-2000.

The neutralizers were used in SCP-2996. So these weren't just things Cimmerian made up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually didn't make those up myself. I'm using items that were already established on the site, though in higher amounts than are probably used in any other SCP to date (because this is containing God).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Y'know, I'm willing to forgive the blatant reality anchors because this paints such a good narrative. I was confused, though, since I come into pages through the Most Recently Created tab and approached these in reverse order. Straightening them out, I like it, I like the personality you gave him and the slow overwriting gimmick with the multiple pages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of like this, but it's going to be a down vote.

Why? Reality anchors and neutralizer whatever you called it. I heavily dislike the use of made up devices to contain things. The main reason I like this site is because it shows how creative people can get with containing anomalous by real world means. Making something up like "reality anchors" ruins any sense of belief I have in the Foundation universe. There has to be a line drawn between what is considered normal and what is considered as anomalous. For me, anything that exists in the real world is normal, and anything that contradicts this is anomalous. By making something that could not exist in reality (let alone never explaining what a reality anchor is or how it functions), you have basically made a SCP to contain a SCP.

Also, going back to the line analogy, if you move the line back to consider this acceptable. At what point does moving the line stop? Why not just have a super powered robot that can teleport and contain anything close to a possible skip in the blink of an eye? At the end of the day, it's just as unbelievable as "reality anchors" for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm an Egypt nerd and you approached this in such a subtle manner that I didn't make the connection.

Bravo. I like it. I liked it even when I didn't quite understand it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hooray, you punctuated y'all correctly! :D

(It's a pet-peeve of mine, as I use it regularly in speech due to regional dialect and thus use it in writing.)

This is more interesting than 343 to me, but I've never really liked 343.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 2273 is similar to a Series I skip, apparently.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh. This caught my eye because "SCP-2343" showed up three times in the Most Recently Created Pages list. It delivered. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In chat, all I heard was the idea. Now made it into this. And this is awesome. +1 for everything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 point to you, wasn't sure anyone would get that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, it sounds to me like a version of SCP-343. This is not a bad thing. Now, i can pretend that it doesn't exist and that this is the one and only old man who is capable of bending reality to his will. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to this note, I came across this: http://www.jstor.org/discover/10.2307/3854180?sid=21105373972101&uid=3738176&uid=2&uid=4

Panepi being Ptah's alias

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: About half-way through the first document I realised the similarities to SCP-343, it was only till I read your spoiler that it kinda ruined the fun for me. It's not a bad article at all, it was just a lot more intriguing when I thought this was a rewrite of "God".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And the first?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, this is the second time this week that someone wrote something Reimagination contest worthy that wasn't for the Reimagining contest. What are the odds?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I got about halfway through writing before I realized how similar(in concept anyway) this was to 343, but then I decided to just own it.

I mean, this is how I imagine the Foundation would actually contain a god of this power level. If you notice, 343 and 2343's altered containment procedures are almost identical, that is a deliberate reference (along with the number).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is the number an intentional reference to, well, 343? Because if it is, I got it, and if it isn't, you should totally act like it is and not let anyone know that it's a coincidence.

I upvoted because of this guy's voice. I'm just hearing an old country boy talking when I read this, and, knowing the whole shebang, that voice makes it much better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Spoilered, as it probably should've been from the start. Glad you liked it, even if you had an issue with that last note. Trying to give away enough without giving away too much is a hard game to play.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got almost none of that from the article, but in a good way of it being incredibly subtle and open to interpretation. My only complaint is that I think the last note explains too much and is too Mr Exposition. Also, I think it'd be more fun if you put your explanation here under a spoiler collapsible =3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Image on all three pages is creative commons from here: http://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Russellshepherd.jpg

SPOILER INSIDE:

+ show block
– hide block

Anyone who is curious, this is Ptah, from the Egyptian Pantheon. He got stuck in Memphis Tennessee after his wife got drunk one night and disappeared, and he went after her to make sure she didn't wreck too much. His wife is Sekhmet… the goddess of war who almost killed everyone in Egypt once.

Special thanks to Decibelle for helping me with cleaning up my final draft.
